blurb: The hardiest peach and one of the last to bloom — the Front Range's best shot at homegrown peaches.
water: Moderate, regular — deep weekly soak in summer
seasons: Pink blossom mid-spring; red-blushed yellow freestone peaches Aug
toxic: Fruit flesh edible; pit, leaves & twigs cyanogenic — toxic to people/pets/livestock
origin: NW China (despite the species name); cultivated since antiquity
habitat: Mountain slopes and forest margins of N and NW China

Ripe fruit — the red-blushed, freestone Reliance peach with its foliage · © RevNaomi (CC BY-NC 2.0) / Flickr
Summer foliage — long lance-shaped peach leaves (species-representative) · © frahome (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

care.hardiness: 'Reliance' is the cold-hardiest of the common peaches — a University of New Hampshire release bred for New England, rated to roughly USDA zone 4/5 and reliably to about -25°F, where most peaches give out by zone 6.[1][2] That makes it Boulder's (USDA 5b–6a) best shot at homegrown peaches, but peaches here are still a gamble: a warm spell followed by a hard April freeze can kill the open flower buds, so a sheltered, well-drained microsite is essential and a crop in any given year is never guaranteed.[1]
care.planting: Plant a dormant bare-root or container tree in spring (April–May on the Front Range), after the worst cold but before leaf-out; dig a hole as deep as the roots and twice as wide, keeping the graft union 2–3 in above the final soil line.[1] Site it on a south- or east-facing slope or against a warm wall for a sheltered microclimate — but avoid the hottest south wall, which pushes earlier bloom into late-frost danger.[1][2] Peaches are poor long-term container subjects on the Front Range: a potted tree's roots are far less cold-hardy than the top and will freeze out in an exposed pot over a zone-5 winter, so use a large (≥20 gal) container, sink or heel it into the ground or move it to an unheated shed/garage for winter, and plan to plant it in the ground within a year or two.[1]
care.sun: Full sun — at least 6, ideally 8, hours of direct sun for good fruit set, ripening, and sugar.[1][2] Intense high-altitude Front-Range sun is welcome; the limiting factor here is cold and late frost, not heat, so favor the sunniest sheltered spot you have.[1]
care.soil: Wants deep, well-drained soil and will not tolerate wet feet — waterlogged ground invites root and crown rots.[1] Boulder's alkaline clay-loam works if drainage is good; peaches prefer a soil pH below about 8.0, and on high-pH soils they are prone to iron chlorosis (yellowing between leaf veins), correctable with chelated iron.[1] Amend heavy clay with organic matter and plant slightly high to shed water.[1]
care.water: Moderate but regular — peaches need a deep soak every 7–14 days through the growing season, wetting the soil 18–24 in deep, for roughly 30 in of water over the season.[1] Consistent moisture during fruit fill (June–August) sizes the crop; let the surface dry between waterings and avoid overhead sprinkling, which spreads foliar disease.[1] Don't let a young tree drought-stress in its first summers.[1]
care.spacing: Give a standard-size tree 12–16 ft of spread; on the Front Range 'Reliance' usually matures around 10–15 ft tall and wide, kept lower by annual pruning.[1] One tree is enough — see propagation/bloom for the self-fertile note.[1]
care.propagation: Cultivars like 'Reliance' do not come true from seed and are propagated by budding or grafting the named scion onto a peach or peach-hybrid rootstock — buy a grafted tree rather than starting from a pit.[1][2] A pit will sprout but yields a random, usually inferior seedling, not 'Reliance'.[2] (Seeds and seedlings also carry the cyanogenic compounds noted under Edible parts.)[2]
care.bloom: 'Reliance' is self-fertile — a single tree sets a full crop without a pollinizer, though bees still improve set.[1] Crucially for the Front Range, it blooms late for a peach: the showy pink blossoms open later than most peach cultivars, which helps the flowers dodge the late-April/early-May freezes that wipe out earlier peaches here.[1][2] Even so, a hard frost after bloom can still cost the crop in a bad year.[1]
care.feeding: Feed lightly. Aim for 12–18 in of new shoot growth a year on a bearing tree; if growth is weak or leaves are pale, apply a modest dose of balanced or nitrogen fertilizer in early spring.[1] Don't over-fertilize — excess nitrogen pushes soft growth, delays hardening before winter, and worsens disease, all of which matter more on a cold-marginal Front-Range peach.[1]
care.maintenance: Peaches fruit on one-year-old wood, so they are pruned hard every year to keep a constant supply of young fruiting shoots.[1][2] Prune in late winter/early spring (February–March, after the coldest weather, before bud swell) to an open-center (vase) form: pick 3–5 outward-angled scaffold limbs 18–24 in up the trunk, keep the center open for light and air, and remove old, shaded, dead, or crossing wood.[1] Thin the young fruit to about one peach every 6–8 in along the branch so the limbs aren't overloaded and fruit sizes up.[1]
care.troubles: Three watch-items on the Front Range. Peach leaf curl (the fungus *Taphrina deformans*) reddens, thickens, and puckers spring leaves — control it with a single dormant copper or chlorothalonil spray applied in late fall after leaf drop or in early spring before bud swell, because once buds open it's too late.[3][4] Peachtree and peach-twig borers tunnel the trunk base and shoots; keep the tree vigorous, watch for gummy frass, and manage with well-timed sprays or trunk treatments.[1][4] Brown rot (*Monilinia*) rots ripening fruit, especially in wet years — strip and discard mummified fruit and prune for airflow.[1][4] Iron chlorosis on alkaline soil and winter/late-frost injury round out the list.[1]
care.harvest: Ripe in August on the Front Range — 'Reliance' is a red-blushed yellow freestone (the flesh parts cleanly from the stone), good fresh and for canning/freezing.[1][2] Pick when the ground color shifts from green to yellow and the fruit gives slightly and smells sweet; tree-ripened peaches are far better than those picked hard, but firm-ripe fruit finishes well on the counter.[1] Watch ripe fruit for brown rot and pick promptly — wildlife takes any peach you leave.[1][4]
care.companions: Underplant with shallow, non-competitive bulbs and pollinator-friendly herbs (chives, alliums) rather than aggressive groundcovers, and keep grass and turf back from the trunk to reduce borer cover and competition for water.[1] Avoid planting where another stone fruit recently died (replant disease) and keep the root zone mulched but the trunk flare clear.[1]

edible.summary: The ripe peach flesh is delicious eaten fresh or cooked, but the stone (pit), seeds, leaves, and twigs are cyanogenic and must not be eaten.[2][3]
edible.parts: Only the ripe fruit flesh is food — 'Reliance' is a freestone, so the sweet yellow, red-blushed flesh lifts cleanly off the central stone for fresh eating, canning, freezing, pies, and preserves.[1][2] The hard stone (pit) it surrounds, the seed inside that stone, and the tree's leaves and twigs are not food.[2]
edible.preparation: Eat the flesh ripe, fresh or cooked; discard the stone rather than cracking it for the kernel.[1][2] Tree-ripe fruit is best, but firm-ripe peaches finish on the counter over a few days.[1]
edible.caution: The stone, the kernel/seed inside it, and the leaves and twigs contain cyanogenic glycoside (amygdalin), which releases hydrogen cyanide when the tissue is crushed or chewed — so do not crack and eat the pit/kernel or chew leaves and twigs, and keep children and pets from doing so.[2][3] The fruit flesh itself is not toxic; the danger is the seed and foliage, and it is greatest when those tissues are ground up or chewed.[2]
